data.title: Bread Financial prices $72.6M depositary shares offering
data.description:
• Bread Financial (BFH) announced the pricing of its underwritten public offering of depositary shares, each representing a 1/40th interest in a share of its Series A non-cumulative perpetual preferred stock, with a liquidation preference of $25 per depositary share.
  • The offering, expected to close on November 25, 2025, is estimated to result in approximately $72,637,500 in net proceeds, which will be used for general corporate purposes.
  • The company expects to apply to list the depositary shares on The New York Stock Exchange.

data.title: Cracker Barrel shareholders vote to keep CEO, but oust director after logo fiasco
data.description:
Cracker Barrel (CBRL) shareholders have voted to keep the restaurant chain's CEO at the helm despite its rebranding debacle, but an independent director failed to secure enough votes to remain on the board.

The company announced that Gilbert Dávila has stepped down and its board has reduced its size from 10 directors to 9 directors.

Shareholders on Thursday voted around 75% of shares to keep CEO Julie Felss Masino on its board, The Wall Street Journal reported. Around 60% of shares voted were against Dávila, a marketing and diversity specialist.

To note, both Masino and Dávila received about 98% of the shares voted at last year's shareholder meeting.

Proxy advisory firms Glass Lewis and Institutional Shareholder Services recommended shareholders vote against Dávila's reelection ahead of the meeting, but they didn't call for Masino's ouster.

Both executives were targeted by activist investors following Cracker Barrel's (CBRL) logo change and store redesigns, moves that were heavily criticized. The company later reverted to its old logo and suspended remodels.

data.title: US lawmakers introduce bill to restrict Chinese chipmaking equipment imports by recipients of CHIPS Act grants: report
data.description:
A bipartisan group of U.S. lawmakers unveiled a bill in the House on Thursday that would restrict the purchase of Chinese chipmaking equipment by CHIPS Act grant recipients for 10 years, Reuters reported.

The bill targets a range of chipmaking equipment, from complex lithography systems, such as those made by ASML (ASML), to machines that slice silicon wafers on which chips are printed, the report added.

The bill was introduced in the House by Republican Jay Obernolte and Democratic member Zoe Lofgren. In December, Democrat Mark Kelly and Republican Marsha Blackburn plan to introduce the bill in the Senate, according to the report.

The CHIPS Act was brought under the Biden administration in 2022 and was aimed at boosting the U.S. chip manufacturing industry, and allocated $39B to spur the construction of new factories and expand existing facilities.

Chip makers like Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), and South Korea's Samsung Electronics (OTCPK:SSNLF) have received grants under the Act. Intel (INTC) also received a grant, but the Trump administration is taking an equity stake in the company. 

China has invested over $40B in the chip industry with a focus on manufacturing equipment. The market share of such equipment has grown substantially, as per background material provided by the lawmakers, the report added.

Among the largest U.S. chipmaking tool companies are  — Applied Materials (AMAT), Lam Research (LRCX), and KLA (KLAC).

The bill also restricts tools from other nations of concern, such as Iran, Russia and North Korea.

There are exceptions in the bill that include the ability for the U.S. to grant waivers if specific tools are not made in the U.S. or by its allied countries.

The bill would only block imports to the U.S. and would not impact the foreign operations of CHIPS Act grant recipients.

data.title: Strategy may face billions in outflows if excluded from MSCI indices - JPMorgan
data.description:
Michael Saylor's Strategy (MSTR) is at risk of being removed from MSCI's (MSCI) equity indices, JPMorgan has warned, which could trigger billions of dollars in outflows.

MSCI (MSCI) is considering the exclusion of bitcoin (BTC-USD) or other digital asset treasury companies from its indices, and has been consulting with the investment community on the matter. 

"MSCI proposes to exclude from the MSCI Global Investable Market Indexes companies whose digital asset holdings represent 50% or more of their total assets," the company said. 

The consultation will remain open until Dec. 31, and a final decision will be announced by Jan. 15, 2026.

Strategy (MSTR) is currently part of the Nasdaq 100, MSCI USA and MSCI World. JPMorgan analysts said out of Strategy's (MSTR) total market cap, ~$9B is likely in passive holdings through ETFs and mutual funds tied to major benchmarks.

If MSCI goes ahead with the exclusion, "outflows could amount to $2.8B… and $8.8B from all other equity indices if other index providers choose to follow MSCI," the analysts warned.

"While active managers are not obligated to follow index changes, exclusion from major indices would certainly be viewed negatively by market participants, raising concerns about the cost and the ability of Strategy to raise equity and debt in the future," they added.

MSTR has slumped 67% since its record high in Nov. 2024, and is down 56% over the last six months. The stock fell 5.7% before the bell on Friday.

data.title: Nvidia-Foxconn's Taiwan supercomputing center to be ready by H1 2026
data.description:
Foxconn Technology (OTCPK:FXCOF) said it plans to build Taiwan’s largest GPU cluster and its first supercomputing center deployment built on Nvidia's (NVDA) GB300 NVL72.

The Apple and Nvidia supplier — which is formally known as Hon Hai Precision (OTCPK:HNHAF) (OTCPK:HNHPF) — said the GB300 NVL72 systems are slated to come online in the first half of next year, marking an acceleration in Taiwan’s sovereign AI infrastructure development. 

Foxconn said the new AI factory infrastructure supports scalable enterprise-grade computing.

The announcement was made at the Hon Hai Tech Day (HHTD25). Hon Hai Technology Group has established Visionbay.ai, a dedicated business unit for AI supercomputing and cloud operations.

The 27-megawatt data center is worth about $1.4B, Reuters reported.

Foxconn and Nvidia did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha about the amount to be spent and the power capacity of the facility. 

Visionbay’s business model centers on an AI Factory ecosystem, offering GPU as a Service, or GPUaaS, compute leasing, NVIDIA-native software solutions, and a cloud-based AI App Store, according to the company.

By getting instant access to high-performance training, fine-tuning, development, and inference capabilities, enterprises can adopt AI with minimal barriers — powered by Nvidia's next-generation accelerated computing architecture, the company added.

Separately, Foxconn announced a partnership with OpenAI (OPENAI) to produce key components for its data centers within the U.S. The Taiwan-based contract manufacturer had teased the announcement related to OpenAI along with its third quarter results, where it provided a bright outlook for next year, pointing to AI-related demand as a big driver of growth in 2026.
